author: @amberyoung1675
text: Have had the good fortune to use both a K40 and a G.forge - the K40 isn't pretty, it does need lots of TLC to keep good cuts, BUT - it is simple and that means so much easier to repair.  Had one part go bust in the Glow (excellent cutting quality) BUT, just lost 5 weeks waiting for a single part to be processed and arrive - a crazy slow support service lost all my winter custom during an already tough year - K40 - simple replacable parts is still better.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
How long does tube last? Power required for different materials? 

I have considered attempting a build of a TEA laser. A long thin spark gap in gas at atmospheric pressure. The spark's plasma serves as an energised lasing medium. Pulsed output... And it's not that difficult to get outputs sufficient to ionise air. So should be able to cut  metal.

author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
text:
How often do you need to remove your lungs and clean out the insides?

Lots of nasty stuff released during cutting, should be filtered and blown outside.  Seems to be a lot of laws about what can come out of an extractor on a commercial building nowadays.  It seems the days of just sucking it up and blowing it out are long gone.

author: @denniskramer9788
text:
Clean allirrors ..

Clean all mirrors same as lens.   Put a piece of masking tape over input side of each mirror... pulse laser to look at position and spot size of beam.  Align upstream mirrors.   Totally agree with earlier air injection at lens comments.    Use focal length to put lens barrel fairly close to work surface.

author: @htmagic
text: RMS, quite informative video! Does threadlocker help keep the mirrors in alignment so they won't jiggle loose from vibrations from the machine?

replies.text: If you nip that up tight it wont need any threadlock, I dont have one of those lasers, but I do have quadcopters and a 3d printer and if you nip the screws up just right they wont fall out.  Dont overtorque them like a 400lb gorilla though.  Hand tight, then a fraction of a turn more.

author: @spehropefhany
text: Interesting. You can get retrofit air-assist nozzles (if that's the right word) for not too much ££. By the way, PVC is not ideal for laser cutting. It releases chlorine gas which is not nice in itself, and that produces hydrochloric acid which makes your nice tools rust.

author: @davegeorge7094
text: You need compressed air lens shroud/upgrade. Air comes out the beam hole at the work. Telescoping beam tubes w/positive pressure keep mirrors clean. Use 12vdc squirrel cage blowers in series air supply, quiet.

author: @ChrisHolden
text: I think a few people have already said - but laser cutting PVC is not a great idea; vapourising the plastic creates toxic fumes (I'm not sure if it *is* chlorine gas) but it's the acidic gunk that's left behind that can be highly corrosive (the vapours too can quickly make a mess of your x-y carriage wheels, which are usually little more than discs of plastic on these cheaper machines). I've had an HPC 3020 for the last ten years or so - which is basically an upgraded version of one of these. Brilliant little machines!

author: @barbufodor1186
text: The deposition is only on that lens? no Issues with the mirrors getting dirty? I am guessing that the cutting plane coincides with the  focal point of the lens, since you have no adjustment in the position of the lens in the holder.

replies.author: @Graham_Wideman
replies.text: Laser cutter usually have a mechanism to raise or lower the bed to position the workpiece at optimum focus position. That height may vary depending on thickness or shape of the workpiece.

replies.author: @barbufodor1186
replies.text: @Graham_Wideman well yes, thank you.  I would expect that.  Unless you know, exactly, where that focal point is and what is the thickness  of the material you cut it would be a pain to determine the optimum position.  I am just guessing here, that the mechanism consists of some optical reading of the thickness material and everything else is adjusted to the fixed focal length of the lens.  If this is not the case than they are complicating the issue without any benefit.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: they do get dirty mate but not as quickly

replies.author: @Graham_Wideman
replies.text: @barbufodor1186 "optical reading of the thickness material". Simpler than that. The laser cutters I've used have a fixed focal length, and a simple hinged or flippable gadget on the carriage that flips down to indicate where the focal point is. You then crank the bed up or down manually (or possibly electrically driven, but using manual up-down buttons) so that the workpiece bumps up against the height gadget. I don't actually recall whether the indicated height is the exact focal point, or rather some distance higher than that so that when it's touching the workpiece, the actual focal point is in the middle of the material. (Obviously in the latter case it would be off for different material thicknesses, but the range of material thickness is quite limited on most low-end laser cutters.)

replies.author: @barbufodor1186
replies.text: @Graham_Wideman even cheaper if you do it manually (costs you nothing). I thought is somehow automated it would not be difficult to do it however you would have at least one more part to clean.
